subtopic_detail: SUDI prevention safe sleep counselling for parents
stem: A 6-week-old healthy term infant attends with her parents for a routine immunisation visit. The parents are tired and report that the baby sleeps best on her tummy on the parental bed, swaddled with a soft cot bumper around her. The father smokes outside but not in the house. Mother breastfeeds at night and occasionally falls asleep with the baby on the couch. Both parents are well, the household has no siblings, and the family lives in a 2-bedroom apartment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 38, SpO2 99% air, T 36.6, weight 5.1 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Pink, well, normal tone, normal cardiorespiratory examination, no concerning features
data_table.Medications: Vitamin D 400 IU daily (breastfed); routine vaccines today

lead_in: Which counselling point will most reduce this infant's risk of sudden unexpected death in infancy?

options.text: Continue tummy sleeping but only on a firm mattress with a thin sheet and remove the cot bumper from the sleep surface
options.rationale: Prone (tummy) sleeping is the single largest modifiable SUDI risk factor; supine sleeping cuts SUDI risk by more than half and is the strongest individual counselling point. (Source: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations)
source_refs: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations

options.text: Advise sleeping with the baby in the parents' bed as breastfeeding is easier and direct contact reduces SUDI risk in young infants
options.rationale: Bed-sharing (especially on couches, with sleep-deprived adults, smokers, or with soft bedding) increases SUDI risk; room-sharing on a separate sleep surface is the recommended arrangement. (Source: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations)
source_refs: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations

options.text: Recommend the father quit smoking entirely as smoke residue on clothing is the most modifiable SUDI risk in this family
options.rationale: Smoking exposure is an important SUDI risk factor and cessation is recommended, but in this family the most modifiable and highest-impact risks are prone sleeping and unsafe couch co-sleeping with maternal fatigue. (Source: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations)
source_refs: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations

options.text: Place the baby on her back to sleep every sleep, on her own safe sleep surface in the parents' room for at least the first 6 months
options.rationale: Supine sleep is the cornerstone of SUDI prevention and pairs with own-surface room-sharing (not bed-sharing) for at least 6 months in the Red Nose evidence-based recommendations. (Source: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations)
source_refs: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations

options.text: Use a wedge or anti-roll positioner to keep the baby on her side during sleep as this is the safest alternative to back sleeping
options.rationale: Side sleeping is unstable (baby can roll prone) and positioners themselves are SUDI hazards; Red Nose advises against any anti-roll or sleep-positioning device. (Source: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations)
source_refs: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations

explanation.summary: Red Nose Australia's six core safe-sleep messages are: sleep baby on back; head and face uncovered; smoke-free environment before and after birth; safe sleeping environment day and night; sleep baby in own safe sleep space in same room as parent or carer for the first 6 to 12 months; breastfeed if possible. Bed-sharing, particularly on couches with sleep-deprived parents and soft surfaces, dramatically increases SUDI risk. Positioners, bumpers, soft bedding, and loose blankets are hazards.
key_points: Supine sleep is highest-impact single intervention.
Room-share (not bed-share) for first 6-12 months.
Firm flat mattress, no bumpers/positioners/loose bedding.
Smoke-free environment, breastfeed if possible.
explanation.pearls: Use an Australian Standard AS/NZS 2172-compliant cot with a firm mattress, fitted sheet only, and feet-to-foot positioning; document safe sleep counselling at every well-baby check until 12 months.

sources.label: Red Nose Australia Safe Sleeping Recommendations
RACGP Guidelines for Preventive Activities in General Practice

subtopic_detail: Paediatric limp Kocher discrimination septic arthritis transient synovitis
stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with 36 hours of limp affecting the right leg. She had a coryzal illness one week ago. She is alert and walking but refuses to bear full weight on the right side. She is mildly distressed only when the right hip is examined; passive log roll provokes pain. There is no joint swelling or warmth, and no obvious limb deformity. She is tolerating oral fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99% air, BP 100/62, T 38.6, weight 16.8 kg
data_table.Examination: Restricted internal rotation right hip, pain with log roll, no effusion or warmth at knee or ankle, no spinal tenderness, distal neurovascular intact
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, CRP 28 mg/L, ESR 42 mm/h; X-ray hip and pelvis normal; bedside ultrasound shows 4 mm right hip effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with simple analgesia and review in 48 to 72 hours, presuming transient synovitis given the recent viral illness and the limited hip effusion
options.rationale: The Kocher count here is 3 of 4 (refusal to weight-bear, fever above 38.5, WCC above 12), giving a 93 percent probability of septic arthritis and requiring arthrocentesis rather than discharge. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

options.text: Trial 48 hours of bed rest with ibuprofen 10 mg/kg three times daily and reassess all Kocher criteria including repeat inflammatory markers
options.rationale: A pre-test probability over 90 percent does not justify watchful waiting; ibuprofen also blunts inflammatory markers and can mask deterioration before arthrocentesis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

options.text: Request an urgent MRI of the right hip and spine before any joint aspiration or commencement of empirical intravenous antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: MRI is helpful when osteomyelitis or pyomyositis is suspected and the joint is dry on ultrasound; here there is a visible effusion and aspiration takes priority because delayed treatment causes cartilage destruction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

options.text: Commence empiric oral cefalexin and arrange outpatient orthopaedic review in the next available clinic within 1 week
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for septic arthritis (which has a 90+ percent pre-test probability here) and a week of delay risks femoral head avascular necrosis; admission and arthrocentesis are required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

options.text: Admit for urgent ultrasound-guided hip aspiration in theatre with paediatric orthopaedics and IV flucloxacillin after fluid sampling
options.rationale: With 3 of 4 Kocher predictors (refusal to weight-bear, fever above 38.5, raised WCC) plus a visible effusion, the probability of septic arthritis is over 90 percent; urgent arthrocentesis under image guidance with antibiotics after cultures is standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

explanation.summary: Kocher criteria differentiate septic arthritis from transient synovitis in a child with hip pain: non-weight-bearing, temperature above 38.5, ESR above 40, WCC above 12 x10^9/L. The probability of septic arthritis is 3 percent (0 of 4), 40 percent (1 of 4), 62 percent (2 of 4), 93 percent (3 of 4), and 99 percent (4 of 4). Aspiration under image guidance is the diagnostic and therapeutic step; antibiotics (flucloxacillin in Australia, plus cefotaxime if under 3 months or risk of MRSA) are given immediately after cultures. CRP and Caird modifications add prognostic value.
key_points: Kocher 4 criteria: NWB, T>38.5, ESR>40, WCC>12.
3 of 4 = 93% probability of septic arthritis.
Aspiration before antibiotics where possible.
Empiric flucloxacillin IV; cefotaxime cover in young infants.
explanation.pearls: Hip aspiration in theatre under fluoroscopy or ultrasound prevents the false-negative of a dry tap; if pus is obtained, send for Gram, culture, cell count and crystals, and arrange surgical washout under the same anaesthetic.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Osteomyelitis and Septic Arthritis

subtopic_detail: Bacterial tracheitis recognition in croup that fails to respond to adrenaline
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to ED with worsening 'croup'. He had 3 days of viral upper respiratory illness with mild barking cough, then today rapidly deteriorated with high fever, severe stridor at rest, copious thick yellow secretions and a hoarse voice. He has had no benefit from two doses of nebulised adrenaline (5 mL of 1:1000) given 30 minutes apart and 0.6 mg/kg oral dexamethasone given on arrival. He is now sitting forward, drooling, looking toxic and frightened. His parents have not refused any care.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 44, SpO2 90% on 2 L/min nasal prongs, BP 96/56, T 39.5, weight 18 kg
data_table.Examination: Toxic-looking, sitting forward, biphasic stridor, suprasternal and intercostal recession, hoarse voice, no rash, no obvious foreign body, copious purulent secretions
data_table.Medications: Two doses nebulised adrenaline, oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, paracetamol given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mL 1:1000 and a second dose of dexamethasone, then reassess in 30 minutes for response
options.rationale: Failure of two doses of nebulised adrenaline plus dexamethasone in a toxic child with purulent secretions and high fever points away from viral croup; further croup therapy delays definitive airway management. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Obtain a lateral neck X-ray to look for a thumb-print sign of epiglottitis or steeple sign of croup before any airway intervention
options.rationale: Imaging in a child with imminent airway compromise delays definitive airway control and has no place ahead of expert intubation; airway team activation is the priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Perform an awake nasendoscopy at the bedside to inspect the supraglottis for swollen cherry-red epiglottis and exclude epiglottitis
options.rationale: Awake examination of the supraglottis in a distressed child with critical upper airway disease risks complete obstruction; airway inspection occurs after controlled anaesthesia in theatre. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Call anaesthesia, ENT and intensive care for theatre intubation, then send tracheal cultures and start intravenous flucloxacillin plus ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis (a viral croup that does not respond to adrenaline, with toxicity, high fever, and purulent secretions) requires senior airway team-led controlled intubation in theatre, then tracheal toilet, microbiological sampling and empirical antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumoniae. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

options.text: Administer high-flow nasal oxygen at 2 litres per kilogram per minute and observe for 1 hour to allow steroid effect before considering intubation
options.rationale: HFNC does not relieve fixed upper airway obstruction from purulent secretions; waiting for steroid effect in a toxic deteriorating child risks loss of the airway. (Source: RCH CPG: Stridor)

explanation.summary: Bacterial tracheitis classically follows a viral URTI with worsening croup that does not respond to nebulised adrenaline or steroids. The child is toxic, febrile, and has purulent tracheal secretions, distinguishing from viral croup (less toxic, dry barking cough) and epiglottitis (sudden onset, drooling, no cough). Management is airway team-led controlled intubation in theatre with simultaneous tracheal toilet, cultures, and intravenous flucloxacillin (Staph aureus is the dominant pathogen in Australia) plus ceftriaxone for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae cover.
key_points: Suspect when croup unresponsive to adrenaline + steroids.
Toxic child, high fever, purulent secretions.
Definitive airway in theatre with anaesthesia + ENT + ICU.
Empirical flucloxacillin + ceftriaxone IV.
explanation.pearls: Tracheal cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus in 50 percent of Australian cases; once intubated, mucus plugs need repeat bronchoscopic toilet and extubation usually proceeds at 3 to 7 days after resolution of secretions and fever.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis acute management
stem: An 8-year-old boy with homozygous HbSS sickle cell disease presents to ED with 6 hours of severe bilateral lower limb and back pain after a cold swimming session at school. His mother gave him paracetamol and ibuprofen at home with minimal effect. He has previously had three admissions for vaso-occlusive crises and is on regular hydroxyurea plus penicillin prophylaxis. He has no fever, cough or breathlessness. He scores his pain 9 of 10. He is alert and orientated, well perfused, with no focal neurology and clear chest examination.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 24, SpO2 97% air, BP 108/64, T 36.8, weight 28 kg
data_table.Examination: Distressed with pain, tender thighs and lumbar region, no joint effusion, normal cardiorespiratory examination, no focal neurology, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (baseline 80), reticulocyte 8 percent, WCC 12.4 (baseline 10), platelets 360, CRP 6 mg/L; venous gas pH 7.39, lactate 1.4
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol and ibuprofen at home; usually hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg/day, phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg BD

lead_in: Within the first hour, what is the most appropriate analgesic and supportive plan?

options.text: Continue oral paracetamol and ibuprofen, then add oral oxycodone 0.1 mg/kg and reassess in 60 minutes after fluids and rest in a quiet bay
options.rationale: Oral analgesia has already failed in a child scoring 9 of 10 pain; sickle cell guidelines mandate strong opioid by intranasal or intravenous route within 30 minutes of arrival. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

options.text: Intranasal fentanyl while siting an intravenous cannula, then commence intravenous morphine titration with maintenance fluids and frequent reassessment
options.rationale: Australian sickle cell guidelines require strong opioid analgesia within 30 minutes (intranasal fentanyl bridge, then IV morphine titrated), warmth, oxygen if hypoxic, and intravenous maintenance fluids without overhydration, with regular reassessment of pain and respiratory status. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

options.text: Arrange urgent exchange transfusion through the haematology service to reduce HbS percentage and abort the vaso-occlusive crisis immediately
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for acute chest syndrome, stroke, multi-organ failure, or priapism; uncomplicated VOC is managed with analgesia, hydration and warmth. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

options.text: Bolus 20 mL/kg of 0.9 percent sodium chloride to suppress sickling and reassess pain after rapid fluid loading is complete
options.rationale: Sickle vaso-occlusive crises are not a hypovolaemic state; large fluid boluses risk pulmonary oedema and acute chest syndrome and do not relieve pain. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

options.text: Administer intramuscular pethidine 1 mg/kg as a single dose to settle the pain rapidly while a cannula is being placed for fluids
options.rationale: Pethidine is contraindicated in sickle cell crises because its metabolite norpethidine accumulates and lowers seizure threshold; IM injections are also painful and slow onset compared with intranasal fentanyl. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

explanation.summary: Vaso-occlusive crises require analgesia within 30 minutes of arrival: intranasal fentanyl 1.5 micrograms/kg as a bridge while siting IV access, then IV morphine titrated (typically 0.05 to 0.1 mg/kg every 10 to 15 minutes), supported by maintenance fluids (avoid overhydration), warmth, oxygen if SpO2 below 95 percent, and continuous reassessment for acute chest syndrome (new infiltrate plus respiratory symptoms requiring exchange). Pethidine is contraindicated. Exchange transfusion is reserved for stroke, acute chest, multi-organ failure, or priapism.
key_points: Strong opioid within 30 minutes (intranasal fentanyl bridge to IV morphine).
Avoid pethidine - norpethidine lowers seizure threshold.
Maintenance fluids; avoid 20 mL/kg boluses.
Exchange transfusion only for chest, stroke, MOF, priapism.
explanation.pearls: Watch for the emergence of acute chest syndrome within 24 to 72 hours of admission: new infiltrate on CXR with fever, hypoxia, or chest pain triggers urgent haematology review for exchange transfusion.

subtopic_detail: Antenatal iron deficiency anaemia oral versus intravenous iron
stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 at 26 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal visit. She is asymptomatic and feels fetal movements well. Her booking bloods showed Hb 112 g/L and ferritin 24 micrograms/L; she was started on oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily 8 weeks ago. Today's repeat shows Hb 105 g/L and ferritin 16 micrograms/L. She reports persistent nausea and constipation that she attributes to the oral iron and admits she takes the tablet only every second day to tolerate it. There is no obvious source of bleeding and she has had a normal cervical screen 12 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 112/72, SpO2 99% air, T 36.6, weight 73 kg, BMI 27
data_table.Examination: No pallor, no koilonychia, fundal height appropriate, fetal heart 142 bpm, no peripheral oedema, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 105 g/L, MCV 78 fL, ferritin 16 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 12 percent, CRP 4 mg/L, B12 and folate normal, normal renal and liver function
data_table.Medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO alternate days (per self), pregnancy multivitamin, folic acid 500 micrograms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange a single intravenous infusion of ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg in the day procedure unit with 30 minutes post-infusion observation
options.rationale: Documented oral iron intolerance, failure to reach therapeutic response, and second-trimester timing make a single dose of IV ferric carboxymaltose (or iron polymaltose) the recommended option in Australia, with monitoring for hypersensitivity. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Increase ferrous sulfate to 325 mg orally three times daily for 4 weeks then repeat full blood count and ferritin to assess response
options.rationale: Tolerance issues prevent adherence already; increasing frequency worsens GI side effects, and Australian guidelines now favour alternate-day or single-dose oral iron with switch to IV iron if not tolerated. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Order a transfusion of 2 units of packed red cells in the day infusion centre with type and crossmatch performed beforehand
options.rationale: Hb is only mildly reduced and she is asymptomatic; transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia or proximity to delivery, not stable iron deficiency early in the third trimester. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Subcutaneous erythropoietin 50 units per kilogram three times weekly for 4 weeks to stimulate erythropoiesis as iron stores recover
options.rationale: Erythropoietin is reserved for renal anaemia or specific scenarios (chronic disease, Jehovah's witness) and is not indicated for routine pregnancy iron deficiency where IV iron is effective. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Switch to oral elemental iron 60 mg daily with vitamin C and a 6-week course of laxatives to address constipation while continuing dietary advice
options.rationale: Persistent intolerance limits adherence; while alternate-day oral iron is reasonable first-line, this woman has already failed an oral trial and her gestation makes IV iron preferable now. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency in pregnancy is treated with oral elemental iron 60 to 100 mg daily or alternate days (better tolerated, equally effective) as first line. IV iron is indicated for intolerance, malabsorption, severe anaemia, failure of oral therapy, gestation late in pregnancy where rapid replenishment is needed, or non-adherence. Australian options are ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg (single dose) or iron polymaltose (slower infusion); both have a low rate of hypersensitivity. RBC transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia near delivery.
key_points: First line: oral elemental iron 60-100 mg alternate days.
IV iron when intolerant, malabsorption, failed oral, late gestation.
Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single dose preferred in AU.
Transfusion only for severe symptomatic anaemia near delivery.
explanation.pearls: Counsel that IV ferric carboxymaltose can cause transient hypophosphataemia (rarely symptomatic) and pseudoanaphylactic Fishbane reactions in 1 to 2 percent; pause infusion if symptoms occur and restart slower.

stem: A 32-year-old woman 3 weeks postpartum after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth attends her GP with a 24-hour history of left breast pain, redness over the upper outer quadrant, fevers and flu-like myalgias. She is breastfeeding her baby on demand and has no nipple cracking, no abscess felt, and no overlying skin necrosis. The baby is feeding eagerly and weight gain has been good. She has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO2 99% air, T 38.6, weight 68 kg
data_table.Examination: Left upper outer quadrant tender wedge of erythema 6 cm, warm, no fluctuance, no nipple cracks, contralateral breast normal, no axillary adenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed; baby feeding well

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous vancomycin in the day infusion unit and arrange daily review until clinical improvement is documented
options.rationale: IV vancomycin is reserved for severe infection, sepsis, treatment failure or proven MRSA; first presentation of community lactational mastitis is treated with oral anti-staphylococcal therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Mastitis)

options.text: Continue regular breastfeeding from both breasts plus paracetamol and ibuprofen, and reassess in 24 hours without commencing any antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: She has systemic illness with fever and tender erythema persisting beyond 24 hours; Therapeutic Guidelines recommend empiric antibiotics for women with fever, tachycardia, and not improving with feeding alone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Mastitis)

options.text: Continue breastfeeding from the affected side with warm compresses and analgesia plus oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 to 7 days
options.rationale: Australian first-line treatment for lactational mastitis with systemic features is continued breastfeeding (start on the affected side), warm compresses, analgesia, and oral dicloxacillin or flucloxacillin 500 mg QID for 5 to 7 days; cefalexin if mild penicillin allergy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Mastitis)

options.text: Refer same day for ultrasound and incision and drainage as a presumed mastitis-associated abscess in the day surgery list
options.rationale: No fluctuance or abscess on clinical examination; ultrasound is indicated if no improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics or a discrete mass is felt, not for first presentation of straightforward mastitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Mastitis)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding from the affected side temporarily and switch to formula feeding while taking oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Continuing breastfeeding from the affected side is recommended to maintain drainage; cessation worsens stasis and risks abscess formation, and flucloxacillin is compatible with breastfeeding. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Mastitis)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis is most often staphylococcal and results from milk stasis and nipple trauma. Management combines continued breastfeeding (start on the affected side; the milk is safe for baby and drainage is therapeutic), warm compresses, analgesia and oral antibiotics if systemic features, persistent symptoms beyond 24 hours, or nipple breakdown. Australian first-line is dicloxacillin or flucloxacillin 500 mg QID for 5 to 7 days; cefalexin for mild penicillin allergy; clindamycin for severe allergy. Lack of improvement after 48 hours prompts ultrasound to exclude abscess.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding from affected side - drainage is therapeutic.
First-line: oral flucloxacillin/dicloxacillin 500 mg QID 5-7 days.
Cefalexin if mild penicillin allergy.
Ultrasound if not improving by 48 h - abscess exclusion.
explanation.pearls: Persistent erythema or a palpable lump after antibiotics in a postpartum woman over 30 years old should be biopsied to exclude inflammatory breast cancer, which can mimic refractory mastitis.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum endometritis empirical broad-spectrum antibiotic choice
stem: A 34-year-old G3P3 is reviewed on the postnatal ward 4 days after a category-2 emergency caesarean section at 39 weeks for prolonged second stage and fetal distress. Membranes had been ruptured for 22 hours and she received intrapartum cefazolin. Since yesterday she has fever, lower abdominal pain, and offensive lochia with increased volume. Her lochia smells foul. She is breastfeeding without difficulty. She has no chest, urinary or wound signs. The caesarean wound looks clean and dry.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 108/68, RR 18, SpO2 98% air, T 38.8, weight 76 kg
data_table.Examination: Soft non-distended abdomen, tender uterus above the umbilicus with bulky fundus, offensive purulent lochia on speculum, no cervical motion tenderness suggestive of PID, wound clean
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4, CRP 138, normal Hb, urine dip clean, blood cultures sent, MSU sent. USS abdomen pending (retained products query)
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 1 g QID; PRN ibuprofen; LMWH prophylaxis as per protocol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g eight-hourly for 5 days plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days to cover anaerobes and atypicals
options.rationale: Cefazolin has poor anaerobic and enterococcal cover and doxycycline is not first-line for endometritis; the polymicrobial pelvic infection needs gentamicin plus metronidazole cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days with outpatient follow-up phone call in 48 hours
options.rationale: Postpartum endometritis with fever, tachycardia and rising inflammatory markers requires inpatient intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics until afebrile, not oral outpatient therapy which has higher failure rates. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Intravenous meropenem 1 g eight-hourly as monotherapy until afebrile then step down to oral cefalexin to complete a 14-day course
options.rationale: Carbapenem monotherapy is excessive for first presentation in an otherwise stable woman and risks selecting resistance; ampicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole is the targeted Australian first line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Intravenous ampicillin 2 g six-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily plus metronidazole 500 mg twelve-hourly until afebrile for 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines for postpartum endometritis use IV ampicillin (or amoxicillin) plus gentamicin plus metronidazole to cover the polymicrobial pelvic flora until afebrile for 24 to 48 hours, then step down to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg eight-hourly as monotherapy with daily review for clinical improvement before any de-escalation step
options.rationale: Clindamycin alone gives inadequate gram-negative cover; standard Australian therapy adds gentamicin (and ampicillin for enterococci) for full polymicrobial coverage. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Endometritis)

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis is a polymicrobial pelvic infection (Gram-positives including enterococci, Gram-negatives, and anaerobes) with risk factors of prolonged ROM, caesarean section, prolonged labour, and intrapartum manipulation. Standard Australian therapy is intravenous ampicillin (covers enterococci) plus gentamicin (Gram-negatives) plus metronidazole (anaerobes) until afebrile for 24 to 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (or doxycycline plus metronidazole for penicillin-allergic) to complete a typical 5 to 10 day course. Check for retained products with ultrasound if no improvement at 48 hours.
key_points: Polymicrobial: needs Gram+/Gram-/anaerobe cover.
IV amp + gent + metronidazole until afebrile 24-48 h.
Step down to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate.
USS for retained products if no improvement at 48 h.
explanation.pearls: Failure to defervesce by 48 to 72 hours despite appropriate antibiotics should prompt USS for retained products, abscess or septic pelvic thrombophlebitis (rare but life-threatening; suspect if persistent fever with normal imaging).

subtopic_detail: Primary genital herpes in pregnancy delivery mode and neonatal prevention
stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 37 weeks 4 days presents to the day assessment unit reporting 24 hours of painful vulval ulceration, dysuria, headache and malaise. She has no past history of genital herpes. Her current partner has cold sores but no known genital lesions. Examination shows multiple tender shallow ulcers on the labia minora with inguinal lymphadenopathy. PCR swabs are taken and confirm primary genital HSV-2 infection 24 hours later (she has been admitted overnight). Membranes are intact, cervix is closed, no contractions, fetal CTG reassuring.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 92, BP 118/72, T 38.0, SpO2 99%; fetal HR 142 reactive
data_table.Examination: Multiple painful vulval ulcers, bilateral tender inguinal nodes, no cervical lesions, intact membranes, cervix closed
data_table.Investigations: HSV-2 PCR positive, HSV-1 PCR negative; HSV serology IgG negative day 1 (consistent with primary infection), pending convalescent titre
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol; topical lignocaine 5 percent gel; no other regular medication

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for delivery?

options.text: Discharge home with reassurance, advise pelvic rest and review only if labour starts or membranes rupture as no specific intervention is indicated
options.rationale: Without antiviral suppression and a delivery plan, the woman is at high risk of vaginal birth on lesions with substantial neonatal HSV transmission; active management is required. (Source: RANZCOG Genital Herpes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan for elective vaginal birth at term with intrapartum intravenous aciclovir during labour and neonatal aciclovir prophylaxis for the baby after birth
options.rationale: Primary genital HSV acquired in the third trimester carries 30 to 50 percent vertical transmission risk at vaginal birth (no protective maternal IgG by delivery); caesarean section is recommended to reduce neonatal HSV. (Source: RANZCOG Genital Herpes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence oral valaciclovir 500 mg twice daily and plan elective caesarean section before 39 weeks if she remains undelivered to minimise vertical transmission
options.rationale: First-episode genital HSV within 6 weeks of expected delivery is managed with suppressive valaciclovir 500 mg BD from diagnosis and elective caesarean before labour or ROM because maternal IgG has not yet seroconverted to protect the neonate. (Source: RANZCOG Genital Herpes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Defer any antiviral therapy until the start of labour then administer aciclovir 800 mg orally five times daily for the duration of active labour only
options.rationale: Suppressive antiviral from diagnosis reduces shedding at delivery; deferring therapy until labour misses the prevention window for primary HSV and does not change the indication for caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Genital Herpes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth but avoid invasive fetal monitoring with a scalp electrode and instrumental delivery to minimise neonatal exposure to ulcerative lesions
options.rationale: Avoiding invasive monitoring helps in recurrent HSV but is insufficient alone in a primary third-trimester episode; caesarean is required because of the high transmission risk. (Source: RANZCOG Genital Herpes in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Primary genital HSV acquired within 6 weeks of delivery carries 30 to 50 percent vertical transmission risk at vaginal birth because maternal IgG has not seroconverted in time to protect the neonate. Management is suppressive valaciclovir (500 mg BD or aciclovir 400 mg TDS) from diagnosis to delivery, plus elective caesarean before labour or ROM. For recurrent HSV at term, suppression from 36 weeks reduces shedding and a vaginal birth is acceptable unless lesions are present at labour. Neonatal HSV (skin/eye/mouth, CNS, or disseminated) is treated with IV aciclovir.
key_points: Primary HSV <6w from delivery: 30-50 percent transmission risk.
Treat with valaciclovir 500 mg BD + elective caesarean.
Recurrent HSV at term: suppress from 36w, vaginal birth ok if no lesions.
Neonatal HSV: IV aciclovir; high morbidity if missed.
explanation.pearls: Always test for HIV alongside HSV because of shared transmission risk; counsel that future pregnancies usually need suppression from 36 weeks even if vaginal birth is then possible.

subtopic_detail: Glycaemic target in frail elderly with hypoglycaemia history
stem:
An 84-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (diagnosed 22 years ago), background mild cognitive impairment, stage 3a CKD (eGFR 48), polypharmacy and one fall in the past month attends his GP for diabetic review. He lives alone with home care twice daily. His current HbA1c is 6.5 percent (48 mmol/mol). He has had two episodes of symptomatic hypoglycaemia (BSL under 3.5 mmol/L) in the past 6 weeks, one requiring family call-out.

His treatment is metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg daily, and basal insulin glargine 14 units nocte. He has no known retinopathy and his most recent foot check was satisfactory.

data_table.Examination: Frail appearance, normal cardiorespiratory, no peripheral oedema, intact monofilament sensation feet, gait slow with frame indoors
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 6.5 percent (48 mmol/mol); eGFR 48; ACR 8 mg/mmol; LFT normal; B12 240 pmol/L; lipids LDL 1.9
data_table.Medications: Metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg daily, insulin glargine 14 units nocte, perindopril 4 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in glycaemic management?

options.text: Continue current regimen but add a continuous glucose monitor for 14 days then reassess with the family at the next routine clinic visit
options.rationale: Symptomatic hypoglycaemia and a fall already document inappropriate over-treatment; treatment de-escalation should not wait for further monitoring data before action. (Source: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults)
source_refs: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults

options.text: Aim for tighter HbA1c at 6 percent (42 mmol/mol) by intensifying basal insulin and adding prandial insulin to reduce risk of long-term diabetic complications
options.rationale: Tight HbA1c targets in frail elderly increase mortality and disability from hypoglycaemia without microvascular benefit at this life-expectancy and disease duration; deintensification is recommended. (Source: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults)
source_refs: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults

options.text: Cease metformin first to reduce gastrointestinal side effects then review the rest of his diabetes regimen in 6 weeks at the next routine appointment
options.rationale: Metformin is not the cause of hypoglycaemia and is well tolerated; ceasing it leaves the gliclazide and insulin (the drivers of hypoglycaemia) untouched and worsens overall regimen. (Source: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults)
source_refs: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults

options.text: Switch gliclazide to a daily SGLT2 inhibitor for cardiorenal benefit and continue insulin and metformin at the same doses with weekly home checks
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors have cardiorenal benefit but bring increased fall risk through volume depletion and increased risk of urinary infection and DKA in frail elderly; first priority is to remove the agent driving hypoglycaemia. (Source: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults)
source_refs: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults

options.text: Cease gliclazide and reduce insulin glargine by 2 units, targeting HbA1c 7.5 to 8.5 percent (58 to 69 mmol/mol) appropriate for a frail older adult at risk
options.rationale: Frail elderly with limited life expectancy, hypoglycaemia and falls have a relaxed HbA1c target of 7.5 to 8.5 percent (58 to 69 mmol/mol); ceasing the sulfonylurea (highest hypoglycaemia risk drug) and reducing basal insulin is the recommended deintensification. (Source: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults)
source_refs: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults

explanation.summary: Australian guidelines individualise HbA1c targets in older adults with type 2 diabetes by frailty, life expectancy, hypoglycaemia risk and patient preference. Healthy older adults target around 7 percent (53 mmol/mol); frail elderly with multiple comorbidities target 7.5 to 8.5 percent (58 to 69 mmol/mol); end-of-life targets focus on symptom avoidance. Sulfonylureas and insulin are the highest hypoglycaemia risk drugs in this group; metformin (if eGFR appropriate) is preferred for ongoing therapy. Deintensify treatment after hypoglycaemia or falls.
key_points: Healthy older adults: HbA1c ~7 percent.
Frail elderly: HbA1c 7.5-8.5 percent.
End-of-life: symptom-focused.
Sulfonylurea + insulin = highest hypoglycaemia risk; cease first.
explanation.pearls: Document and act on every symptomatic hypoglycaemic episode in elderly diabetes patients; one severe hypoglycaemic event doubles 5-year mortality risk and is a stronger indicator than any HbA1c value.

sources.label: Diabetes Australia: Glycaemic Targets in Older Adults
RACGP General Practice Management of Type 2 Diabetes

subtopic_detail: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes acute treatment
stem: A 62-year-old man with stage 4 CKD (baseline creatinine 280, eGFR 18) on long-term perindopril and spironolactone presents to ED with 2 days of vomiting, diarrhoea, and now muscle weakness and palpitations. He has not been eating but has continued his usual medications. He looks unwell, mildly hypovolaemic and dyspnoeic. On examination he is alert. The bedside ECG taken 5 minutes ago shows broad-based QRS at 152 ms with tall symmetrical peaked T waves in all precordial leads and absent P waves. Urgent VBG shows potassium 7.4 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 58, BP 102/64, RR 22, SpO2 96% air, T 36.4, weight 78 kg
data_table.Examination: Sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, JVP not seen, chest clear, no focal neurology, generalised proximal weakness
data_table.Investigations: VBG K 7.4, Na 134, HCO3 16, lactate 1.9, ionised Ca 1.10. Creatinine 412, eGFR 12; ECG: peaked T, broad QRS 152 ms, absent P
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 8 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, frusemide 40 mg, paracetamol PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate first step in management?

options.text: Intravenous 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes by slow push with continuous cardiac monitoring throughout the administration
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemic ECG changes with QRS widening require immediate myocardial membrane stabilisation; IV calcium gluconate 10 percent 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes acts within minutes and is given before any potassium-shifting therapy. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Recommendations)

options.text: Activate the renal registrar for emergency haemodialysis as the first definitive treatment while no other temporising measure is attempted yet
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is the definitive treatment but takes time to mobilise; immediate temporising treatment (calcium then insulin-dextrose) cannot be deferred while waiting for vascular access and dialysis circuit setup. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Recommendations)

options.text: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g orally as a one-off dose to remove potassium from the gut while awaiting renal replacement therapy review
options.rationale: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate is slow (hours) and not first-line for life-threatening hyperkalaemia; it does not stabilise the myocardium and risks bowel necrosis in unwell patients. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Recommendations)

options.text: Intravenous actrapid (soluble) insulin 10 units in 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose over 15 minutes as the first intervention before any other therapy
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium into cells over 30 to 60 minutes but does not stabilise the myocardium; calcium must be given first when ECG changes are present. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Recommendations)

options.text: Salbutamol 10 mg nebulised over 10 minutes as a single agent treatment while ECG monitoring continues with bedside cardiac team
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol shifts potassium intracellularly but takes 30 minutes to act and is not enough in a haemodynamically unstable patient with QRS broadening; cardiac stabilisation comes first. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Recommendations)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia (>6.5 mmol/L or any value with ECG changes) is a medical emergency. The sequence is: cardiac monitoring, IV calcium (10 percent gluconate 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes or 10 percent chloride 10 mL via central line) to stabilise the myocardium, then potassium-shifting therapy with insulin-dextrose (10 units actrapid plus 50 mL 50 percent dextrose), nebulised salbutamol 10 mg, and consideration of sodium bicarbonate if metabolic acidosis. Definitive removal is haemodialysis or correction of cause (cease ACE inhibitor and spironolactone, treat hypovolaemia). Calcium binds to cardiac sodium channels within minutes but does not lower the potassium.
key_points: Calcium first if ECG changes (stabilises myocardium).
Then insulin-dextrose + salbutamol (shifts K intracellularly).
Definitive: dialysis, cease offending drugs.
Repeat ECG and potassium after each intervention.
explanation.pearls: Effect of calcium gluconate lasts only 30 to 60 minutes; repeat if QRS does not narrow within 5 to 10 minutes, and document the potassium and ECG before and after each treatment step to track response.
